statements.statement: Every word depends on the words before it.
statements.imageAlt: Diagram showing how AI reads your context and predicts the most likely next word from many possibilities
statements.explanation:
AI does not understand your question and then write a reply. It predicts one word at a time. It looks at all the words that came before, and picks the most likely next word. Then it looks at everything again, including the word it just picked, and predicts the next one. This keeps going until the full response is built.

This means every word AI has seen so far shapes what comes next. The previous words are the context. Change the context, change the output.

This is why what you type matters so much. Your words are the context AI uses to predict everything that follows.

experiment.intro: Open ChatGPT and try these three prompts in separate conversations:

prompts.label: Prompt A
prompts.text: Write a paragraph about a bank.

prompts.label: Prompt B
prompts.text: I am a fisherman. Write a paragraph about a bank.

prompts.label: Prompt C
prompts.text: I am a financial advisor. Write a paragraph about a bank.

experiment.result:
Notice how the same word "bank" produces completely different results. In prompt A, AI could go either way. In prompt B, it writes about a river bank. In prompt C, it writes about a financial bank.

You did not change the question. You changed the context. And that changed everything.

children.statement: It does not know when it is wrong.
children.explanation: Because AI is just predicting the next word, it has no idea if what it wrote is true or false. A correct fact and a made up fact look exactly the same to it. It will cite a book that does not exist with the same confidence as a real one. Always verify anything that matters.

terms.hallucination: When AI confidently writes something that is completely made up. It is not trying to lie. It just cannot tell the difference between real and fake.

children.statement: It learned from everything we wrote, good and bad.
children.explanation: AI was trained on billions of pages written by people. That includes great writing, but also stereotypes, outdated views, and plain wrong information. It does not filter any of this out. It just picks whatever pattern fits best.

terms.bias: When AI consistently leans one way because the text it learned from leaned that way. It reflects human writing, the good parts and the bad parts.

statements.statement: The app matters more than the AI.
statements.explanation: ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini all use similar technology underneath. What makes them feel different is the app built around it: the instructions it follows, the tools it connects to, how it handles your conversation. Two apps using the same AI model can give you completely different experiences.

children.explanation: "Write something about dogs" gets you a generic paragraph. "Write a 3 sentence bedtime story about a golden retriever who learns to fly, for a 5 year old" gets you something useful. The more specific your instructions, the better the result. This is the single most useful skill you can build.

children.statement: You can adjust how creative it gets.
children.explanation: There is a setting called temperature. Low means predictable, focused answers. High means creative, surprising answers that sometimes go off track. Most apps set this for you, but knowing it exists explains why AI sometimes feels random.
On its own AI can only write text. With plugins it can browse the web, read your files, check your calendar, run code. Plugins are what turn a chatbot into a useful work tool.

statements.explanation: AI does not remember you between conversations. Every new chat is a blank slate. It does not know what you discussed yesterday, what you prefer, or that correction you made five minutes ago. If an app seems to remember you, that is the app saving notes and feeding them back. The AI itself has zero memory.

children.statement: There is a limit to how much it can hold at once.
children.explanation: Your messages, its replies, any files you uploaded, its instructions, all of this has to fit in a fixed space. Go past the limit and it starts forgetting what was said at the beginning of your conversation.

children.statement: More information does not mean better answers.
children.explanation: Pasting a 50 page document does not help. AI pays the most attention to the beginning and end of what you give it, and tends to miss things in the middle. Keep your inputs focused and relevant.

statements.explanation: Vague question gets a vague answer. A specific request with context and examples gets a genuinely useful result. Learning to write clear instructions is the one skill that makes everything else with AI work better.

children.explanation: Instead of describing the format you want, paste two or three examples. AI picks up patterns instantly. Show it how you want emails to sound, how reports should look, what style you prefer. Examples beat explanations every time.

children.statement: Use it for first drafts, not final versions.
children.explanation: AI is fast at getting you a starting point. Let it write the first version, then edit with your own knowledge. You skip the blank page problem and keep your expertise in the result.
AI does not know your audience, your context, or your standards. You do. AI brings speed. You bring the knowledge that turns generic output into good work. Neither is useful alone.

statements.statement: Some AI does things, not just writes.
statements.explanation: Most AI just generates text. But newer AI can actually take actions: book meetings, fill out forms, write and run code, search through your files. This changes things because actions have real consequences.

children.statement: Agents figure out the steps on their own.
children.explanation: An assistant answers one question and waits. An agent takes a goal and works through the steps itself. You say "plan my trip to Tokyo" and it searches flights, checks hotels, looks at your calendar, and comes back with options. Useful, but always review what it did.

children.statement: You can describe what you want and AI builds it.
children.explanation: People are building apps by describing what they want in plain words. AI writes the code. This works surprisingly well for simple things. But when something breaks, someone still needs to understand the code to fix it.

statements.statement: It works with more than just text.
statements.explanation: AI can look at photos and describe what is in them. It can listen to audio and create transcripts. It can work through math problems step by step. These abilities are new and improving fast.

children.statement: Show it a photo, get an answer.
children.explanation: Take a photo of a plant and ask what species it is. Screenshot an error on your screen and ask for help. Photograph a menu in another language and get it translated. This is one of the most practical things AI can do and most people have not tried it.

children.statement: Some models reason before they respond.
children.explanation: Newer models can slow down and think through a problem before answering. They are better at math, logic, and anything that needs careful reasoning. They take longer and cost more, but for hard problems the extra thinking is worth it.
You can upload your own documents, reports, or notes and ask questions about them. This is how AI stops giving generic answers and starts being useful for your specific work.

statements.explanation: AI is fast but not reliable. Read that email before you send it. Verify that number before you use it. Review that report before you submit it. Five minutes of checking can save you from a very embarrassing mistake.

children.statement: Most of what you hear about AI is exaggerated.
children.explanation: That impressive demo might have taken 50 tries. That amazing tool might only work for one specific task. Be curious and try things, but do not trust headlines. The best way to understand AI is to use it yourself and see where it actually helps.
